title: What does the full lifecycle of an LLM system look like, and where do failures typically cluster?
prompt:
What does the full lifecycle of an LLM system look like, and where do failures typically cluster?

*Answer in the editor as you would out loud in an interview (a few sentences to a short paragraph is fine). Then use 💡 Hint, 🧠 AI feedback, or 📗 Show answer.*
reference_solution:
The lifecycle is longer than most teams plan for: (1) Scoping — define testable terms, (2) Prompts — iterative test case design, (3) Integration — RAG, tools, memory, (4) Offline eval — establish baselines, (5) Rollout — canary and shadow mode, (6) Monitoring — quality, cost, latency, (7) Iteration — steady-state tuning, (8) Deprecation — model migrations. Failures cluster at predictable transition points: at deployment, systems are often built against a happy-path eval set that doesn't reflect real user query variation; at scaling, no monitoring plus a small eval set means the prompt fails once it hits production volume; and with provider updates, the underlying model changes silently degrade quality with no regression detection in place.

**Takeaway:** Treat the eval set and monitoring as living infrastructure across the whole lifecycle, not a one-time gate before launch, since failures tend to show up exactly at the transitions teams stop paying attention to.
hint_notes: 8 stages: scoping, prompts, integration, offline eval, rollout, monitoring, iteration, deprecation. Failures cluster at deployment, scaling, and silent provider updates.
